claim: A 2024 study by Petley et al. found that rainfall-triggered landslides cause over 4,000 deaths annually, with South Asia — particularly Nepal, India, and Bangladesh — accounting for over 50 percent of global landslide fatalities.
method: Reviewed global landslide fatality database, trigger analysis, regional breakdown, and data-completeness assessment for South Asian countries.
limitations: Landslide-fatality data undercounts events in remote Himalayan valleys where reporting infrastructure is sparse.
The 50 percent figure for South Asia is an aggregate — individual country and district fatality rates vary substantially.
Fatality data is event-based — it does not capture the chronic risk from slow-moving landslides or progressive slope deformation.
The database combines rainfall-triggered and earthquake-triggered landslides — disentangling triggers for attribution requires event-specific analysis.

claim: InSAR from Sentinel-1 at 5-20m resolution can detect ground deformation preceding slope failure, providing days to weeks of warning potential before catastrophic landslides in suitable terrain, but performance is limited in steep, forested, cloud-covered Himalayan terrain.

source.title: Sentinel-1 InSAR for landslide monitoring — ESA documentation

method: Reviewed Sentinel-1 InSAR documentation for deformation-detection methodology, spatial resolution, revisit cycle, and performance limitations in mountainous terrain.
limitations: InSAR coherence is lost in vegetated terrain — forest cover in Himalayan valleys reduces deformation-detection capability.
Steep slopes create geometric distortions (layover, foreshortening) that limit InSAR accuracy in the steepest Himalayan terrain.
Cloud cover does not affect SAR (radar penetrates clouds), but atmospheric water vapor introduces phase delays that can be mistaken for ground deformation.
The 6-12 day revisit cycle limits warning lead time — deformation occurring between passes may be missed for rapid-onset failures.

claim: NASA's Global Landslide Hazard Assessment models map rainfall-triggered landslide susceptibility at 1km resolution, but validation in Himalayan terrain is limited — steep slopes, cloud cover affecting satellite precipitation, and sparse ground data reduce model accuracy in the regions where landslide risk is highest.
method: Reviewed NASA landslide hazard model documentation, resolution specifications, validation studies, and Himalayan terrain performance assessment.
limitations: The 1km resolution smooths micro-topographic slope variations critical for landslide initiation in Himalayan terrain.
Rainfall-trigger inputs depend on satellite precipitation products (GPM, CHIRPS) that have reduced accuracy in mountainous terrain with complex orography.
Validation in Himalayan countries is limited — most validation studies are from lower-relief terrain where model performance is better.
Susceptibility maps are probabilistic — they estimate relative likelihood, not absolute probability or timing of specific landslides.

claim: Nepal's Department of Hydrology and Meteorology has a landslide early warning pilot program, but coverage is limited to a few districts and the warning-to-evacuation pipeline has not been systematically tested against actual landslide events.

source.title: Nepal Landslide Early Warning System — Pilot Assessment

method: Reviewed DHM pilot program documentation, coverage assessment, warning-dissemination methodology, and community-response evaluation.
limitations: The pilot covers only a few districts — national-scale coverage is years away even with sustained investment.
Warning-to-evacuation effectiveness has not been tested against a major landslide event — the pipeline exists on paper but community response is unverified.
Rainfall-threshold triggers for the pilot are based on regional data that may not capture micro-climate variation within districts.
Sustainability depends on continued funding and institutional capacity — both are uncertain in Nepal's resource-constrained context.
